answers.value:
Participant, Ms. Margaret Louise Henderson (DOB 1942-03-08), reports concerns regarding the way her morning medications were administered and how staff communicated with her during her visit to the Senior Care Partners PACE day center on 2026-01-15.

According to Ms. Henderson, she arrived at the center around 8:30 AM and was seated in the main activity room. She states that she usually receives her morning medications by 9:00 AM because she has been instructed by her primary care provider to take her blood pressure medication and her diabetes medication with a small snack to avoid feeling lightheaded. On this particular day, she reports that her medications were not brought to her until approximately 10:45 AM, which is significantly later than her usual time.

Ms. Henderson reports that she reminded a staff member (a nursing assistant whose name she did not know) around 9:30 AM that she had not yet received her medications. She states that the staff member responded, "We’re busy right now; you’ll get them when we get to you," in a tone that she perceived as dismissive and abrupt. She reports feeling embarrassed because this was said in front of other participants in the activity room, and she felt as though she was being scolded for asking about her medication. She states that this made her reluctant to ask again, even though she was starting to feel shaky and anxious.

Around 10:30 AM, Ms. Henderson reports that she began to feel lightheaded and slightly nauseated. She states that she mentioned this to another staff person at the front desk, who then contacted the nurse. The nurse reportedly came to her within about 10 minutes, checked her blood pressure and blood sugar, and then administered her medications along with a snack. Ms. Henderson recalls that her blood sugar was "a little high" and her blood pressure was "higher than it usually is," though she does not remember the exact numbers. She denies passing out or falling but states that she felt "off" and needed to rest in a recliner for about 20–30 minutes.

Ms. Henderson’s primary concerns are: (1) the delay in receiving her scheduled medications compared to her usual routine, (2) the lack of clear communication about why there was a delay, (3) the tone and manner in which the first staff member responded to her request, and (4) feeling that her health needs and comfort were not taken seriously. She reports that this is not the first time her medications have been late, though she says this is the latest they have ever been and the first time she has felt physically unwell as a result.

She states that she values the PACE program and generally likes the staff, but she would like reassurance that her medications will be given within an appropriate time window and that staff will respond respectfully when she asks questions about her care. She also wants documentation that she has raised this concern, in case similar issues occur in the future.

answers.value:
On 2026-01-16, the grievance was formally received and documented by Angela Martinez, MSW, Participant Care Coordinator, after Ms. Henderson requested to speak with someone about her experience the previous day. Angela met with Ms. Henderson privately in a quiet office at the center to allow her to describe the situation in detail. During this meeting, Angela actively listened, clarified the timeline of events, and confirmed Ms. Henderson’s primary concerns: delayed medication administration, perceived disrespectful communication by a staff member, and anxiety about potential health consequences.

INITIAL REVIEW AND FACT-FINDING:
1. Chart Review: Angela reviewed Ms. Henderson’s electronic health record, including her current medication list and usual administration times. The MAR (Medication Administration Record) indicated that morning medications are typically scheduled between 8:30 and 9:30 AM. On 2026-01-15, the MAR showed administration at 10:47 AM, which was later than the usual window and consistent with Ms. Henderson’s report.

2. Staff Interviews: Angela spoke with the RN assigned to the day center that morning, as well as the nursing assistant believed to have interacted with Ms. Henderson.
   - The RN acknowledged that staffing was short that morning due to a last-minute call-in, and that medication passes were running behind. The RN stated that no one informed participants proactively about the delay.
   - The nursing assistant recalled Ms. Henderson asking about her medications but did not remember the exact wording used. The assistant acknowledged that she may have sounded rushed and stated, "I was trying to move quickly and probably came across short. I didn’t mean to be rude, but I can see how it might have felt that way to her." She also stated that she did not immediately relay Ms. Henderson’s reminder to the nurse because she was focused on assisting another participant with transfers.

3. Vital Signs and Incident Check: Angela reviewed vital sign readings from that day. The nurse had documented that Ms. Henderson’s blood pressure was mildly elevated and her blood sugar slightly above her usual range at the time medications were finally given. There was no documentation of a fall or injury, and Ms. Henderson’s symptoms improved after receiving medications and a snack. No emergency transfer was required.

FINDINGS:
- The grievance was found to be substantiated with respect to delayed medication administration and insufficient communication. The medication pass occurred significantly later than the participant’s typical window, and there was no proactive explanation provided to Ms. Henderson about the delay.
- The concern regarding staff communication and tone was partially substantiated. While the staff member did not recall intending to be disrespectful, she acknowledged that she may have spoken abruptly while under time pressure. Given Ms. Henderson’s report and the staff member’s acknowledgment, the concern about perceived disrespect was considered valid.

ACTIONS TAKEN TO ADDRESS THE CONCERNS:
1. Immediate Participant Support:
   - Angela apologized directly to Ms. Henderson on behalf of the program for the delay in her medications and for the way the communication made her feel. She validated Ms. Henderson’s feelings and emphasized that participants are encouraged to speak up about their care needs.
   - Angela and Ms. Henderson discussed strategies for future situations, including encouraging Ms. Henderson to ask to speak with the nurse or a supervisor if she feels her concerns are not being heard.

2. Staff Coaching and Education:
   - The nursing assistant involved received one-on-one coaching from the RN supervisor on 2026-01-18. The discussion focused on respectful communication, maintaining a calm and reassuring tone even when busy, and the importance of promptly relaying participant concerns about medications to the nurse.
   - The RN supervisor also provided a brief in-service during a team huddle on 2026-01-19 for all day-center nursing staff, emphasizing:
     • The need to inform participants when there will be a delay in medication administration.
     • The importance of acknowledging participant concerns without dismissive language.
     • Documentation expectations when there is a significant deviation from usual medication timing.

3. Process Improvements:
   - The team implemented a simple visual tracking tool for morning medication passes so that if there is a staffing shortage or delay, the RN can more quickly identify participants who are at risk if medications are given late (e.g., those with diabetes or significant blood pressure issues).
   - A procedure was reinforced whereby, if medication passes are running more than 30 minutes behind schedule, a staff member or the RN will make a brief announcement in the activity room and/or approach participants individually to explain the delay and provide an estimated timeframe.

4. Service Delivery Request (SDR):
   - An SDR was initiated to update Ms. Henderson’s individualized care plan. The care plan now specifically notes:
     • Target time frame for her morning medications (ideally between 8:30 and 9:00 AM when possible).
     • Her preference to be informed if medications will be more than 30 minutes later than usual.
     • Her history of feeling lightheaded when medications are significantly delayed, prompting staff to monitor her more closely if delays occur.

FOLLOW-UP WITH PARTICIPANT:
- On 2026-01-22, Angela met again with Ms. Henderson to review the findings and the actions taken. This feedback was provided orally during her center visit, as requested, and an official letter summarizing the grievance and the resolution steps was prepared and mailed to Ms. Henderson’s home address the same day.
- During the follow-up meeting, Ms. Henderson stated that she appreciated the apology and the thorough explanation. She reported that on her subsequent visits, her medications had been given closer to her usual time and that staff had been more communicative about any delays. She stated that she felt "more comfortable" continuing to attend the center and that she was satisfied with the resolution as long as the new procedures continue to be followed.

CONCLUSION:
The grievance regarding delayed medication administration and unsatisfactory communication was substantiated. Corrective actions included staff coaching, clarification of expectations, minor workflow changes to monitor medication timing, and an update to the participant’s care plan via an SDR. The participant has been informed both orally and in writing and has indicated that she is satisfied with the steps taken at this time.
